head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots decorated heteroatom co-doped core-shell Fe0@POCN for degradation of tetracycline via multiply synergistic mechanisms

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, antibiotics have been emerging as seriously concerning environmental pollutants that were widespread in aqueous environments (Li et al., 2018a, 2018b). Tetracycline (TC), one of the broad-spectrum synthetic antibiotics, has been the most widely used in medical treatment and animals feeding. Overuse and incomplete treatment processes result in negative effects on human health and environment (Yu et al., 2019). Methods commonly used to remove tetracycline in water, include biological method, physical method ( Peng et al., 2018), chemical method (Duan et al., 2019) and photocatalytic method ( Li et al., 2018a, 2018b ). Compared with other methods, photocatalytic method has the advantages of high catalytic efficiency, low energy consumption, strong stability, and complete degradation without secondary pollution. In this way, photocatalytic method attracted extensive attention for its considerable application prospect.

Graphite carbon nitride (GCN) is a new type of visible light photocatalyst, which has the advantages of simple preparation method and low precursor price, which can easy to synthesized by use melamine, urea, thiocarbamide, etc. At the same time, it can absorb part of visible light from solar light, due to the suitable valence and conduction band positions. In this way, GCN has received extensive attention and presented broad potential in the future (Zhao et al., 2018; Hu et al., 2019). It is found that GCN can be used to degrade various organic pollutants, produce hydrogen and reduce carbon dioxide, presenting attraction for the existing energy and environmental problems ( Jiang et al., 2018; Pang et al., 2018). However, GCN cannot be applied widely in industry due to the drawbacks. One of the defects is large band gap (2.7 eV), which occurred only when the visible light wavelength was less than 459 nm in solar light can be absorbed. The second defect is that photo-generated electron-hole pairs are easy to recombine. The last one is that the material is difficult to separate solid from liquid after photocatalytic reaction, which may cause secondary pollution to the ecological environment (Luo et al., 2019).

In our previous studies, efforts had been port towards introducing magnetic nano-particles Fe3O4, CoFe2O4 into GCN, so as to solve the problem of solid-liquid separation after photocatalytic reaction. In addition, researchers have also tried to combine GCN with magnetic materials such as NiFe2O4(Zhang et al., 2019b), CoMoO4(Xiang et al., 2019) and ZnFe2O4 ( Lu et al., 2018). Among many magnetic materials, nano zero-valent iron (Fe0) has been extensively studied because of its strong magnetism. At the same time, it has extremely high redox activity and could act as the electron acceptor to active oxidizing agent, which can degrade organic pollutants or convert valence states of inorganic pollutants ( Zhou et al., 2017 ).

Herein, we proposed a facile method to synthesize a core-shell catalyst which was introduced Fe0 nanoparticles in the co-doped by phosphorus and oxygen GCN, which further decorates by CQDs to build ternary heterostructure photocatalyst (Fe0@POCN/CQDs) for the degradation of tetracycline. Then, a new Fe0@POCN/CQDs/PS system was proposed. The mechanism of photocatalytic performance enhancement was as follows: firstly, doping and modifying graphite carbon nitride can improve the defects of the material; secondly, the combination of magnetic material nano zero-valent iron can solve the problem of solid-liquid separation of photocatalyst, meanwhile, the oxidation-reduction activity of Fe0 itself can act as the electron acceptor to activate continuously persulfate system and highly efficiently assist in the degradation of TC to achieve the synergistic degradation effect; finally, the multiply synergistic effects due to the dopant of carbon dots and the introduction of Fe0 nanoparticles can significantly improve the optical properties of the composite material, broaden the absorption range of the catalyst for visible light. In this paper, the degradation pathway of tetracycline by Fe0@POCN/CQDs was studied under the irradiation of visible light, and the multiply synergistic mechanism of photocatalytic action was summarized.
Detailed information of sources and purities of used materials were shown in Supplementary Information.

Melamine was used as a precursor, melamine polyphosphate was used as a phosphorus and oxygen source. The substances were subjected to ultrasonic treatment for 0.5 h in 50 mL deionized water at a ratio of 1:1, and then dried at 80 °C for 24 h. Then the mixture was ground into powder and transferred to a crucible with a cover, and was placed in a muffle furnace to be heated at 550 °C for 3.0 h at a heating rate of 3 °C/min. The resulting mixture being cooled to room temperature and was ground into powder, and phosphorus and oxygen co-doped graphite carbon nitride were successfully prepared (POCN).

Firstly, the composite photocatalyst Fe0@POCN was synthesized by a self-assembly method. The specific steps were as follows: 1.0 g of prepared POCN and 0.1 g of Fe0 were respectively ultrasonically dispersed in 200 mL of anhydrous ethanol and 100 mL of hexane, and then the mixed solution was ultrasonically treated for 3 h. And the prepared materials were collected by a centrifugal method. This prepared mixture was finally dried at 90 °C for 24 h, which was denoted as Fe0@POCN.

Secondly, CQDs was prepared via the typical hydrothermal method. Briefly, 6.0 g citric acid and 2.0 g urea were combined in a beaker under magnetic stirring and dissolved in 15 mL distilled water. Then the mixed solution was transferred to a stainless steel autoclave and reacted for 5 h at 180 °C. After cooling to room temperature, the large particles were removed by centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 30 min. Subsequently, the CQDs solution was dried overnight at 80 °C to obtain CQDs powder.

Finally, 50 mg of CQDs and 1 g of Fe0@POCN were added to 30 mL of ethanol, transferred to a water bath, and reacted at 60 °C for 2 h. Then, the mixture was calcined at 300 °C under N2 atmosphere for 3 h to obtain ternary heterojunction doped with quantum dots, denoted as Fe0@POCN/CQDs.
The specific steps were shown in Supplementary Information.

In this study, the photocatalytic degradation of TC was conducted. The detailed description was as following: a 500 W Xe lamp was used as a visible light source, and ultraviolet light was filtered out by a 420 nm filter. During the experimental, 30 mg of photocatalysts were added to 50 mL of simulated wastewater with a TC concentration of 15 mg/L, and stirred in the dark for 30 min at a speed of 150 r/min until the adsorption-desorption equilibrium at 25 °C. Then the samples were taken every 10 min, and TC concentration in the solution was measured at 357 nm so as to obtain a photocatalytic degradation rate.

Effects of different of Fe0@POCN/CQDs dosage (20, 25, 30, 35, 40 and 45 mg) on degradation of TC were carry out in pH was 7. To investigate the Fe0@POCN/CQDs pH adaptability, effects of the initial pH (3, 5, 7, 9, 11 and 13) of the solution on the photocatalytic performance were carried out, and the dosage of Fe0@POCN/CQDs was 30 mg. All of the samples were taken every 10 min to obtain a photocatalytic degradation rate. The degradation rate of TC can be calculated from Eq. (1):
(1)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of microstructure and chemical properties of photocatalysts nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared materials were characterized by TEM, and the results were shown in Figs. S1(a)–(e). These is uniformly bulk stacked structure can be observed in Fig. S1(a), which is a typical graphite carbon nitride structure ( Cao et al., 2019). Fig. S1(b) showed the microstructure of the prepared nano zero-valent iron. It can be observed that the zero-valent iron particles present spherical particles with a diameter of 10–100 nm and uniformly disperse in the carbon nitride frameworks. The TEM image of POCN (Fig. S1(c)) presented many pore structures can be observed on the surface of POCN compared with GCN. The developed pore structure of material surface mainly due to phosphorus and oxygen co-doped, which meant that more active sites can be provided for photocatalytic reaction. As shown in Fig. S1(d), the microscopic structure of showed a typical core-shell structure. It can be observed that the Fe0@POCN material was composed of large sphere-like structure on the scale of micrometer and the large Fe0 particles around 25 nm, which indicated the successful preparation of core-shell photocatalyst. The size of Fe0 core in the catalyst is about 40 nm. The TEM image of Fe0@POCN/CQDs was shown in Fig. S1(e). As we can see, it can be observed that Fe0 spherical particles are incorporated into the POCN from the image, and simultaneously the structure of the scarce dots of 5–10 nm in diameter were appeared on POCN nanosheets from nano CQDs, the incorporation of CQDs are loaded onto POCN nanosheets, but not exposed to Fe0 nanoparticles, providing an ideal construction of ternary heterostructure, which could aﬀect charge separation and the optical absorbance of the as-prepared photocatalyst ( Li et al., 2019 ). The mapping diagrams of the prepared material Fe0@POCN/CQDs were shown in Figs. S1(f)–(k). From the diagrams, it can be observed that the catalyst consists of C, N, O, P and Fe elements, and C, N, O and P elements are uniformly distributed on the entire surface of the material, while Fe elements are only distributed in the core of the catalyst, indicating that the core-shell combined structure of the catalyst has been successfully prepared.

Magnetization curves of the catalyst were shown in Fig. S2(b). The maximum magnetization (Ms) of Fe0@POCN and Fe0@POCN/CQDs can reach 15.07 and 12.06 emu/g, respectively. The parameters of residual magnetization and coercivity of the materials can be obtained from the Fig. S2(b). Coercivity of Fe0@POCN and Fe0@POCN/CQDs are 15.11 Oe and 2330.61 Oe, respectively. They were all measure to be within the soft magnetic range (<102 A/m), indicating that the materials are ideal magnetic catalysts. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of as-prepared photocatalysts were given in Fig. S3(a). GCN, POCN, Fe0@POCN and Fe0@POCN/CQDs are exhibit type IV isotherms with type H3 hysteresis loops at high P/P0 of approximately 0.6–0.9, indicating a mesoporous structure in the catalysts. The SSA of GCN, POCN, Fe0@POCN and Fe0@POCN/CQDs are 7.56, 52.67, 37.21 and 28.36 m2/g, respectively. Besides, the pore size distribution of prepared catalyst was displayed in Fig. S3(b). As we can see, the pore size of the material is mainly distributed at 2–10 nm.

In this study, XPS spectra were used to study the bonding forms of various elements in Fe0@POCN/CQDs. The survey spectrum was shown in Fig. S4. From the spectrum, it can be observed that C, N, O, P and Fe were the major elements detected for the Fe0@POCN/CQDs. The XPS spectrum of C1s can be fitted as three peaks (Fig. 1 (a)), and the three peaks centered at 284.8 eV, 288.3 eV and 288.8 eV respectively. The binding energy is located at 284.8 eV corresponding to C–C bond, and the peak centered at 288.3 eV originates from sp2 C atom (NC-(N)2) bonded to nitrogen in the aromatic ring. However, the peak centered at 288.8 eV was the bond energy of O–CO (Zhou et al., 2015). As shown in Fig. 1(b), the XPS spectrum of N 1s was deconvoluted into three peaks centered at 398.9 eV, 399.7 eV and 401.2 eV corresponding to the bond energy of N atom sp2 hybridization in the C–NC structure, the bond energy of N-(C)3, and the bond energy of N–H.

After deconvolution, the O 1s peak of Fe0@POCN/CQDs was divided into three peaks ( Fig. 1(c)), the above common peaks with binding energies at 530.2 eV, 531.6 eV and 532.8 eV respectively. Wherein the binding energy at 530.2 eV corresponds to the O-M bond of metal atom and oxygen atom, the peak at 531.6 eV corresponded to the O–H bond in H2O, and the peak at 532.8 eV corresponded to the O–C bond. The P 2p peak splitting result of catalyst Fe0@POCN/CQDs was shown in Fig. 1(d), and the binding energy has peaks at 133.7 eV and 134.5 eV respectively. The peak at 133.7 eV corresponds to the PN bond, which was formed by substituting carbon atoms in the material structure with phosphorus atoms and covalently bonding with adjacent nitrogen ( Hu et al., 2018). The peak at 134.5 eV corresponds to the bond energy of PO, which is attributed to the thermal condensation of phosphorus and oxygen element at high temperature during polymerization ( Deng et al., 2017 ). According to the fitting result of Fe 2p spectrum (Fig. 1(e)), the two peaks at 706.9 eV and 718.9 eV corresponded to Fe0 (Liu et al., 2018), and the peaks at 710.3 eV assigned to FeO, and peaks at 712.8 eV and 7124.1 eV correspond to Fe3+ in Fe2O3. From the Fig. 1(e), it can be observed that the peaks belonging to Fe0 were weak, while the peaks of Fe2+ and Fe3+ due to the contact of the material with oxygen in the air and the formation of FeO and Fe2O3 on the surface of Fe0. Accordingly, a unique structure of nano-Fe0 particle was formed in which nano Fe0/FeO/Fe2O3 particle wrapped (Wu et al., 2019). In general, the closely contacted interfaces with strong interaction are essential in forming ternary heterostructure, which would be benefit for inhibition of recombination rate of photogenerated electron–hole pairs and promotion the charge separation as well as the transportation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic performance of different as-prepared catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The degradation performances of GCN, POCN, Fe0@POCN and Fe0@POCN/CQDs were evaluated by degradation of TC under visible light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), similar TC degradation curves were obtained during the degradation process. And the TC degradation efficiency of GCN, POCN, Fe0@POCN and Fe0@POCN/CQDs were 18.2%, 32.8%, 53.9% and 86.7%, respectively. Without using any modification method, the degradation of TC was only 18.2% within 60 min in the case of pure GCN. However, other modified samples including doping elements and matching with semiconductors, the photodegradation activities toward to TC were significant enhanced, which can be ascribed to the increased of content of oxygen containing functionalities and structure defects that prevented the recombination of photoexcited hole-electron pair. Besides, Fe0@POCN/CQDs has the best photocatalytic performance, which was 4.76 and 2.64 times higher than those of GCN and POCN.

In order to evaluate the degradation performance of TC by photocatalysis, the obtained data were fitted with a pseudo-first-order kinetic model ( Hu et al., 2019), and the pseudo-first-order kinetic equation was shown in Eq. (2):
(2)
ln
(
C 0
/ C )
= k t
where C0 is the concentration of the initial pollutant in the solution (mg/L), C is the concentration of the pollutant at time t (mg/L), k is the degradation rate constant (min−1), and t is the reaction time (min).

Fig. 2(b) displayed the pseudo-first-order kinetic model, it can be observed that TC photodegradation reactions followed first-order kinetics closely (R2 > 0.95). It can be clearly observed that the photocatalytic rate of ternary hybrids was superior to binary hybrids or individually structure, indicating the efficient ternary heterostructure contact among components is important for realizing excellent photocatalytic performance. As shown in Fig. 2(b), the photocatalytic degradation rates of TC by the GCN, POCN, Fe0@POCN and Fe0@POCN/CQDs were 0.0033 min−1, 0.0066 min−1, 0.0129 min−1 and 0.0336 min−1, respectively. The degradation rate constant of Fe0@POCN/CQDs was approximately 10.18 times faster than that of the pure GCN, which was higher than previous research (Shao et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of initial pH value of solution on photocatalytic performance
Effect of catalyst dosage on photocatalytic performance
Effect of multiple anions on photocatalytic performance
ce:section.ce:para: In order to consider the application of catalysts in the treatment of municipal sewage, some substance (coexisting anions and natural organic matter) exist in water should be considered. In this paper, the major inorganic anions in aquatic environments including nitrate ions (NO3 −), chloride ions (Cl−), bicarbonate ions (HCO3 −) and phosphate ions (PO4 3−) have been taken into consideration. Different concentrations of NO3 −, Cl−, HCO3 − and PO4 3− (0.1 mM, 0.5 mM, 2.0 mM) were added into the TC solution and then degraded by the Fe0@POCN/CQDs. The results were shown in Fig. S5. It can be seen that the addition of NO3 − has little effect on the TC remove efficiency (Fig. S5(a)), the degradation of TC was decreased from 86.7% to 83.9% with the increase of NO3 − to 2.0 mM, indicating that the NO3 − had the lowest reaction rate with •OH or SO4 •−compared with other ions. By comparison, the coexisting of Cl− showed obvious inhibition of the TC photodegradation (Fig. S5(b)), which would react with the radicals and inhibit the catalytic performance in radical-based reaction (Duan et al., 2019). The TC remove efficiency gradually decreased from 86.7% to 60.9% with the increase of Cl− addition (from 0 to 20 mM). This could be due to the exhaustion of •OH or SO4 •− radicals by Cl− could react with •OH or SO4 •− to form less active chlorine radicals species (Cl• or HClO•−), resulting in the lower TC removal efficiency. In addition, Cl− could be oxidized by PMS and then generated less reactive chlorine species. Similarly, the presence of HCO3 − and PO4 3− also inhibitory catalytic performance (Fig. S5(c-d)), mainly due to HCO3 − and PO4 3− could increase the solution pH to inhibit the production of radicals (Wei et al., 2016; Yin et al., 2019). With the concentration of HCO3 − increased from 0 to 2.0 mM, the remove efficiency TC by Fe0@POCN/CQDs was decreased to 76.3%. The existence of HCO3 − would consume the hydroxyl/sulfate radicals, which was usually used as a radical scavenger in AOPs. It was reported that the presence of PO4 3− might strongly inhibit the production of oxidation species due to the occupation of reaction sites (Yang et al., 2014), resulting in the decreased photodegradation of TC by Fe0@POCN/CQDs.

The regenerability assessment of Fe0@POCN/CQDs was also studied in this experiment. As can be seen from Fig. 3 (a), the degradation efficiency of TC almost remained unchanged after 5 regeneration cycles, indicating that the photocatalyst has excellent stability (Zhao et al., 2020a, 2020b). The full wavelength scanning diagram of TC solution after reaction for 0min, 10min, 20min, 30min and 60min were shown in Fig. 3(b). It can be seen that the absorption peak intensity of the solution gradually decreased as the reaction process, indicating that Fe0@POCN/CQDs can effectively remove the concentration of organic pollutants. The TC removal efficiency and total organic carbon removal efficiency of GCN, POCN, Fe0@POCN and Fe0@POCN/CQDs for comparison (Fig. 3(c)). It can be observed from the figure that the TC removal efficiency can reach 18.2%, 32.8%, 53.9% and 86.7%, respectively, with the corresponding mineralization degradation efficiency of the TOC were 12.6%, 26.5%, 43.9% and 76.5% after reaction of 60 min, respectively. It was found that the mineralization of TC for the photocatalyst process in consistent with the variation of TC oxidation efficiency. Besides, it is worth noting that the mineralization efficiency of Fe0@POCN/CQDs was 6.07 times than that of GCN. In addition, it can be observed that Fe0@POCN/CQDs has almost no difference in XRD patterns after five cycles of TC degradation (Fig. 3(d)), which further indicated that Fe0@POCN/CQDs has good practical application in contaminated water and maintains excellent photocatalytic performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization the PS hybrid activation system by response surface methodology (RSM)
ce:section.ce:para:
Persulfate with high oxidation potentials are attracting increasing attention in organic pollutants remove (Yin et al., 2019). However, the slow decomposition rate of persulfate is the main drawback. In this study, the combination of Fe0@POCN/CQDs and visible light were used to construct a hybrid activation system to accelerate the decomposition of PS. To optimize the operational conditions, response surface methodology (RSM) was studied with a central composite design (CCD). And the three main parameters (concentration of PS, initial pH value and temperature of the solution) denoted as X1, X2 and X3 were carried out by RSM. Table S1 represents the higher and lower levels of the parameters. The quadratic model was used to fit the experimental data, final equation in terms of coded factors follows as:
(3)

( % )
= 82.60 + 9.08

− 0.35

+ 0.13
X 1 2
− 5.93
X 2 2
− 2.02
X 3 2
where, X 1, X 2 and X 3 represent the concentration of PS, initial pH value and temperature of the solution, respectively.

An analysis of variance (ANOVA) was used to assess the adequacy of the model, and the parameters were shown in Table S2. According to the analysis of variance of the regression model by Eq. (2), P (Prob > F) < 0.0001, indicating the model term has higher significance and is suitable for the optimization research of these operation parameters. The influence of the three single factors on the TC removal effect of the catalyst is order as: temperature > concentration of PS > initial pH value. The degree of interaction between concentration of PS and temperature of the solution in the secondary term is the largest. And the degree of interaction between initial pH value and temperature of the solution was the smallest. In the equation, X 2 2 and X 3 2 were considered as representative significant levels. The mismatch term was used to evaluate the fitting degree between the model and the experiment, and P value of the mismatch term was 0.4789 (>0.05), indicating that the mismatch of the model is not significant and the fitting is statistically significant.

As shown in Table S2. It can be seen that F value is 68.85 and multiple regression coefficient R2 = 0.9883, indicating that the 98.83% removal rate change can be explained by this model, which showed that this model fits the actual situation well. Model P > F<0.0001(P>F < 0.05 is regarded as significant), indicating that this model was highly significant and can be used to predict response values. Correction decision coefficient Adj R2 = 0.9733, indicating that 97.33% variability of experimental data can be explained by this regression model. Adeq Precision means that the ratio of effective signal to noise, and the precision of this experiment was as high as 27.912 (>4), which is considered reasonable (Uddin and Baig, 2019).

According to the regression quadratic equation obtained by fitting, the contour lines and 3D surface plots were obtained ( Fig. 4
). As can be seen from Fig. 4(a) and (d). When pH was determined, the removal rate of TC by Fe0@POCN/CQDs is gradually increased with the increase of PS concentration. This may be due to the fact that with the increase of PS concentration, more sulfate radicals can be generated after full contact with photocatalyst, which can rapidly produce degradation of TC. When the PS concentration was constant, the removal of TC by Fe0@POCN/CQDs increased firstly and then decreased with the increase of pH value. When the pH was lower and in an acidic environment, the scavenging effects of H+ ion on ·OH limited the removal rate of TC. At the same time, acidic conditions were not conducive to the activation of persulfate by photocatalyst, resulting in slower rate of sulfate radical generation. When the solution under alkaline pH, the high OH− concentration tended to generate the active oxide species quencher CO3 2− under alkaline conditions, which reacted with the active species generated in the photocatalytic process and resulted in the inhibition of photocatalytic performance ( Zhang et al., 2018b ).

Fig. 4(b) and (e) illustrated the interactive effect of temperature and concentration of PS on the TC removal by Fe0@POCN/CQDs. It was observed from the graphs that both the factors showed the significant interaction on photocatalytic efficiency of TC. As shown in Fig. 4(b), the removal efficiency of TC improved with increase of the PS concentration. When the PS concentration is more than 2 mM, the removal of TC by Fe0@POCN/CQDs could maintain above 90% over the entire temperature range (15–65 °C). It is obvious that the removal of TC by photocatalyst was suppressed under the higher temperature. It was mainly due to that high temperature will inhibit the activation rate of PS and affect the yield of free radicals. The interactive effects of temperature and initial pH value on the TC removal by Fe0@POCN/CQDs were given in Fig. 4(c) and (f). It is obvious that increasing pH and decreasing initial pH value lead to an increasing in removal of TC. The removal of TC by Fe0@POCN/CQDs will be more than 76% at the initial pH value of 7 over the entire temperature range. The contour lines and 3D surface plots simulated by Design-expert software showed that there are maximum points. In this investigation, the optimum parameters were designed to be 4.69 mM of PS concentration, 10.52 of pH and 15.71 of temperature respectively, which could obtain 97.57% TC removal efficiency according to 3D surface plots and contour lines best optimized values for the factors (Uddin and Baig, 2019).

The absorption properties of the photocatalyst were characterized by UV–vis-DRS spectra, and the results were shown in Fig. 5 (a). It can be seen that all materials have strong absorption ability to visible light. Compared with GCN the absorption ranges of POCN, Fe0@POCN and Fe0@POCN/CQDs for visible light had red shift. Among of all catalyst, Fe0@POCN/CQDs had the strongest absorption ability for visible light, corresponding to its strongest photocatalytic performance, which was consistent with the results of previous experiments. By converting UV–vis-DRS spectra into (Ahv)2-hv spectra, Kubelka-Munk curves can be obtained (Fig. 5(b)). From the diagram, the band gap of GCN, POCN, Fe0@POCN and Fe0@POCN/CQDs were approximately 2.71 eV, 2.65 eV, 2.58 eV and 2.55 eV, respectively. It is worth noting that the band gap of Fe0@POCN/CQDs was obviously reduced. Compared with GCN, the absorption range of visible light was red shift from 457.6 nm to 486.3 nm, which can significantly enhance the photocatalytic performance of the material. The XPS-VB spectra of prepared photocatalysts were described in Fig. 5(c). The valence bands of GCN, POCN, Fe0@POCN and Fe0@POCN/CQDs were 1.69 eV, 1.36 eV, 1.71 eV and 1.49 eV, respectively. According to the relationship between conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) ( Duan et al., 2020 ), the CB of GCN, POCN, Fe0@POCN and Fe0@POCN/CQDs are −1.02 eV, −1.29 eV, −0.87 eV and −1.06 eV, respectively. According to the data calculated above, the band structure diagrams of the as-prepared materials can be obtained (Fig. 5(d)).

The PL spectra of GCN, POCN, Fe0@POCN and Fe0@POCN/CQDs were described in Fig. S6 (a), with an excitation wavelength of 375 nm during measurement. It can be observed that GCN had the strongest fluorescence peak among all materials, indicating that electron-hole pairs generated by GCN under the action of visible light have very high recombination rate ( Chen et al., 2019). The fluorescence peak intensities of POCN, Fe0@POCN and Fe0@POCN/CQDs are significantly decreased in comparison with the GCN, which further confirmed that the composite material can effectively inhibit the recombination of photogenerated electron holes. The result was consistent with the conclusion reached by photocatalytic experiments.

To further investigate the photochemical properties of the photoexcited charge carriers of the catalyst, the photocurrent response curves was presented in Fig. S6(b). It can be clearly observed that the photocurrent density of POCN, Fe0@POCN and Fe0@POCN/CQDs are all enhanced compared with GCN. The maximum photocurrent densities of POCN, Fe0@POCN and Fe0@POCN/CQDs were 1.88, 2.40 and 3.38 times higher than that of GCN. The increase in the photocurrent density of Fe0@POCN/CQDs meant that the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers was much higher than those of GCN, which was similar with the result of the PL analysis. As can be seen in Fig. S6(c), the electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) was used to study the electrical conductivity of catalyst. And the order is as follows: GCN > POCN > Fe0@POCN > Fe0@POCN/CQDs. As shown in Fig. S6(b), Fe0@POCN/CQDs possesses apparently smallest arc radius compared with other catalysts, suggesting it has the lowest charge transfer resistance, which further verifying the effectiveness of the hybridization structure via multiply synergistic effects on the improving the photocatalytic activity (Zhao et al., 2020a).

To elucidate the active radicals involved in the degradation of TC by Fe0@POCN/CQDs, sodium oxalate (SO), p-benzoquinone (BQ) and isopropyl alcohol (IPA) were used as scavengers for active radicals (Yu et al., 2020). Fe0@POCN/CQDs exhibits a high degradation rate for TC, which can reach 86.7% without the addition of any scavengers (Fig. 6 (a)). However, the removal rate of TC reduced to 47.7%, 57.9% and 61.2% when SO, BQ and IPA were added to the solution, respectively. Therefore, the order of active radicals contribute to the degradation of TC was holes (h+)>superoxide radicals (•O2 −)>hydroxyl radicals (•OH). These results further demonstrated that h+, •O2 − and •OH played pivotal roles in the degradation of TC by Fe0@POCN/CQDs during the photocatalytic reaction.

In order to verify the experimental results, electron spin resonance (ESR) was used to reveal the active radicals in the photocatalytic reaction system. Superoxide radicals (•O2 −) and hydroxyl radicals (•OH) were detected on Fe0@POCN/CQDs at room temperature. In Fig. 6(b), •O2 − characteristic peak could be found under the irradiation of visible light. However, DMPO-•O2 − signal was not observed when the sample was in dark condition, which indicated that •O2 − could be generated under the irradiation of visible light. Similar to the former, DMPO-•OH signal was not observed in Fe0@POCN/CQDs when it was in dark condition, and characteristic peak could be observed obviously after 10 min of illumination (Fig. 6(c)). These results confirmed that both of •O2 − and •OH radicals are produced in the photocatalytic degradation process on Fe0@POCN/CQDs, which was consistent with the conclusion of capture experiment.

Additionally, the main active factors involved in the hybrid activation system were studied by ESR. The characteristic peaks of DMPO-•OH and DMPO-SO4 •− radicals were identified in Fig. 6(d). Compared with Fig. 6(c), the distinct signal of DMPO-SO4 •− can be found as the persulfate added. Similar to the former, DMPO-•OH and DMPO-SO4 •− signal were not detected when the hybrid activation system was without irradiation of visible light. This meant the radicals of •OH and SO4 •− radicals were hardly generated without visible light radiation (Lu et al., 2020). Consequently, it is convictive that the contribution of SO4
•- and •OH reactive species played a vital role in PS system.

In this study, the degradation mechanism of tetracycline by the catalyst and its composite system were shown in Eqs. (4)–(16). When without adding persulfate to the photocatalytic system, Fe0@POCN/CQDs can generate a large number of e− and h+ under the irradiation of visible light, and the e− could react with dissolved oxygen in water to generate •O2 − with strong oxidation. At the same time, h+ would react with water to generate •OH ( Du et al., 2020 ). When persulfate was added to the photocatalyst system, the photocatalyst and persulfate could construct a composite advanced oxidation system. In Fe0@POCN/CQDs-PS system, persulfate could be decomposed under the irradiation of visible light to generate SO4 •-. Photogenerated electrons generated by Fe0@POCN/CQDs under the irradiation of visible light also participate in the decomposition of persulfate, but the activation rate of both was slow and only a small part of SO4 •- can be generated. In the whole system, Fe0 will react with persulfate, and the generated Fe2+ has extremely strong activation performance to persulfate, meanwhile Fe2+ will be oxidized to Fe3+. The key step for the whole system to continuously and efficiently activate persulfate was that the generated Fe3+ will be reduced to Fe2+ by Fe0, and then the persulfate will be continuously activated to generate sulfate radical.

As the same time, Fe0@POCN/CQDs-PS system degraded TC via non-radical oxidation process. Singlet oxygen (1O2) could be converted from O2 •- in the present of PS under acidic conditions. HO2 • radicals mainly generated by the activation of PS could react with HO• . The consumption of HO2 • to form O2 •- and 1O2. Besides, O2 •- could also react with HO• to produce 1O2 under alkaline conditions ( Duan et al., 2019). Due to the oxidation potential of carbon-activated PS complexes determined by the quantity of PS adsorbed, a large amount of PS adsorbed on POCN can result in a higher redox potential. Therefore, TC will be decomposed by surface electron transfer over the graphite carbon skeleton of POCN, while the equilibrium potential of the Fe0@POCN/CQDs-PS system exceeds the oxidation potential of TC. It should be mentioned that the activation process of PS to generate the reactive oxygen species O2 •-, 1O2 and HO• and the surface electron transfer process were the accounts for the obtained outstanding performance of TC. The possible degradation mechanism was shown in Fig. 7 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: The possible degradation pathway of TC by Fe0@POCN/CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to reveal the proposed degradation pathway in the photodegradation of TC by Fe0@POCN/CQDs, the HPLC-MS technique was used to investigate the generated intermediates at different reaction times. Fig. S7 presented the mass spectra of TC at the reaction time of 0 min, 10 min, 30 min and 60min. According to the results, the possible degradation intermediate products were marked by the number from B to F (Fig. S7). Firstly, the CC-(C)2 fragment or C–CO fragment is the initial attacking site for tetracycline, we deduce that the production of compound A (m/z = 445) firstly fragmented into B (m/z = 433) by ring-opening reaction, which will provide more reaction sites for Fe0@POCN/CQDs ( Wang et al., 2018). Then, after a series of losing side-chain groups, the intermediate products C (m/z = 349) and D (m/z = 301) with lower molecular weight were produced. Then, as the photocatalytic degradation reaction going on, intermediate products E (m/z = 267) and F (m/z = 223) were produced under sequential redox reaction. Finally, TC and intermediate products were completely degraded into CO2 and H2O.

In this study a facile method has been demonstrated to fabricate in-situ carbon quantum dots and co-doping in GCN matrix to improve the photocatalytic property of novel core-shell catalyst. The results from the photocatalytic experiments showed that the degradation efficiency of TC by Fe0@POCN/CQDs could reach 86.7%, which was 4.76 times higher than that of GCN. The significantly enhance the photocatalytic performance of the material mainly due to the band gap of Fe0@POCN/CQDs was reduced from 2.71 eV to 2.55 eV, so the absorption range of visible light was red shift from 457.6 nm to 486.3 nm compared with GCN. At the same times, the maximum photocurrent densities of Fe0@POCN/CQDs was 3.38 times higher than that of GCN, which meant that photogenerated carriers has higher separation efficiency. According to the ESR analysis, the active species of h+, •O2 − and •OH play pivotal roles in the degradation of TC by Fe0@POCN/CQDs during the photocatalytic reaction. Besides, the catalyst combined with PS system is more efficient for TC degradation, which could obtain 97.57% TC removal efficiency under the optimum parameters. In addition, HPLC-MS was used to reveal the generated intermediate products and proposed TC degradation pathway by Fe0@POCN/CQDs, and the possible degradation pathways of TC photodegradation were proposed.

Xiaoming Peng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing, Review & Editing. Wendong Luo: Investigation. Methodology, Writing - Original Draft，Visualization. Jianqun Wu: Investigation. Methodology.
